title: Quantum geometric anomalous Hall response in orbitally nonunitary superconductors
authors: Viktor Frilen, Annica M. Black-Schaffer, Ankita Bhattacharya
abstract: We investigate the anomalous Hall response (AHR) in a multiband superconductor at optical frequencies, a phenomenon intimately related to the polar Kerr effect, a key probe of time-reversal symmetry breaking in superconductors. In translationally invariant multiband systems with purely intraband pairing, Galilean invariance decouples center-of-mass and relative motion of Cooper pairs, leading to the widespread expectation that a finite AHR requires either disorder or finite interband pairing amplitudes. However, this restriction can be lifted by the quantum geometric effects inherent to multiband Bloch states. Using a honeycomb lattice tight-binding model with Kane-Mele spin-orbit coupling, we analyze the AHR for the time-reversal symmetry broken chiral $d$-wave spin-singlet and chiral $p$-wave equal-spin-triplet pairing states with intraband pairing only. We demonstrate, through both analytical and numerical calculations, that the spin-singlet state yields a vanishing AHR, even with its broken time-reversal symmetry, whereas the equal-spin triplet state exhibits a finite AHR, even when it is spin-unitary. We attribute the latter to orbital nonunitarity, which, in the presence of spin-orbit coupling, generates the spin-polarized Bogoliubov quasiparticle states required for a finite AHR. The response is mediated by interband velocity matrix elements governed by the quantum geometry. This finding establishes that spin-unitary, but orbitally nonunitary pairing, can generate a finite AHR even without interband pairing and thereby revises the criteria for Kerr signals in superconductors.
summary: This theoretical work examines the anomalous Hall response in multiband superconductors using a honeycomb lattice model. The key finding is that orbital nonunitarity, even when spin-unitary and restricted to intraband pairing, can generate a finite AHR via quantum geometric effects. This establishes a new pathway for detecting time-reversal symmetry breaking signals in superconducting materials.
main_problem: The study investigates the anomalous Hall response (AHR) in multiband superconductors at optical frequencies, specifically determining if a finite AHR can arise from spin-unitary but orbitally nonunitary pairing when only intraband pairing is considered.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that for equal-spin triplet pairing, a finite AHR persists even with purely intraband pairing, attributing this to orbital nonunitarity interacting with SOC. This revises the criteria previously thought necessary for observing Kerr signals in superconductors.
method: The analysis employs Kubo linear response theory applied to the Bogoliubov-de Gennes Hamiltonian within a multiband framework. Calculations involve analyzing Berry curvature terms derived from intraband pairing contributions.
model_or_system: The system is modeled as a multiband superconductor on a honeycomb lattice, incorporating Kane-Mele spin-orbit coupling. The superconducting state is described by investigating chiral d-wave spin-singlet and chiral p-wave equal-spin triplet pairing states.
key_observables: Anomalous Hall Response (AHR) or anomalous Hall conductivity ($\sigma_H(\omega)$), related to the polar Kerr effect.
important_parameters: Optical frequency ($\omega$), Kane-Mele SOC strength, and the degree of orbital nonunitarity ($v$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes intraband pairing contributions only by setting interband pairing terms to zero in certain parts of the Hamiltonian. The study focuses on specific prototype TRS breaking states.
figures_summary: Figures compare AHR for d-wave singlet and p-wave triplet pairings under varying conditions, notably contrasting results with and without interband pairing contributions.
paper_structure: The paper builds from establishing the theoretical framework using BdG Hamiltonians and Kubo response theory. It then analyzes specific pairing symmetries (singlet vs. triplet) to show how orbital nonunitarity can generate a finite AHR even when conventional mechanisms (like interband coupling) are suppressed.
why_interesting: The finding that spin-unitary but orbitally nonunitary pairing can induce an AHR without interband coupling provides a new, purely intrinsic mechanism for probing broken symmetries in superconductors, which is highly relevant to topological superconductivity research.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Real (red) and imaginary (blue) part of the anoma- lous Hall conductivity σH as a function of optical frequency ℏω normalized with t for chiral d-wave spin-singlet pairing on the honeycomb lattice with Kane-Mele SOC (a) with in- terband pairing and (b) without interband pairing. Here λSOC = 0.05t, µ = −0.5t, ∆0 = 0.1t, and temperature kBT = 0.05t.
FIG. 1. Momentum-space distribution of q± in Eq.(9) for the chiral d-wave (η = 1) spin-singlet pairing state for ∆0 = 0.1t.

FIG. 3. Real (red) and imaginary (blue) part of the anoma- lous Hall conductivity σH as a function of optical frequency ℏω normalized with t for chiral p-wave equal-spin triplet pair- ing on the honeycomb lattice with Kane-Mele SOC for (a) interband pairing and κ = 0, (b) interband pairing and κ = 1, (c) no interband pairing and κ = 0, and (d) no interband pair- ing and κ = 1. Other parameters are the same as in Fig. 2.
